source: Plain-language practice; the passive that hides who is accountable
article: An internal review found that mistakes were made in the accounting of the housing voucher program, leading to about 4 million dollars in payments that should not have been authorised.

critiques.text_quote: mistakes were made in the accounting
critiques.question: 'Mistakes were made' is the classic dodge. Who made them? If the review names an official or an office, name them. If it does not, say the review has not yet identified who.
critiques.why_it_matters: Passive voice with no actor lets the institution off the hook for a 4 million dollar error. The reader cannot tell if this is one clerk or a systemic failure, and that difference is the whole story.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Name the accountable party if the review identifies one. If it does not, say so plainly rather than leaving a faceless 'mistakes were made'.
critiques.replacement: the review found that the program's accounting was mishandled, though it has not yet identified who approved the payments

source: Plain-language practice; acronym density that loses the reader
article: HUD wrote to NYSHCR this week over how HCR has overseen NYCHA's federal funding, citing a backlog of audit findings.

critiques.text_quote: HUD wrote to NYSHCR this week over how HCR has overseen NYCHA's federal funding
critiques.question: Four agency acronyms in one sentence: HUD, NYSHCR, HCR, NYCHA. By the end of the clause the reader has lost track of who is writing to whom about whom. Could a reader who does not work in housing follow this?
critiques.why_it_matters: Three or more acronyms in two sentences make a story unreadable for anyone not already on the beat. A story about public housing oversight needs to be readable by public housing tenants.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Keep one acronym per paragraph at most. Replace the rest with plain descriptive titles.
critiques.replacement: The federal housing department wrote to the state housing agency this week over its oversight of the city's public housing authority, citing a backlog of audit findings

source: Plain-language practice; technical units that hide the scale of the story
article: The Federal Reserve cut its benchmark rate by 25 basis points on Wednesday, the first reduction in 18 months.

critiques.text_quote: cut its benchmark rate by 25 basis points
critiques.question: 'Basis points' is invisible to a reader who does not follow finance. What does 25 of them mean for someone with a mortgage? The scale of the story is hidden inside a term most readers skip.
critiques.why_it_matters: A unit the lay reader cannot decode makes the size of the news invisible. The standard is to translate jargon like this every time it appears outside a specialist section.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Translate it in line. 25 basis points is a quarter of a percentage point. Better still, say what that does to a typical payment.
critiques.replacement: cut its benchmark rate by 25 basis points, a quarter of a percentage point,

source: Plain-language practice; the news buried beneath the procedure
article: The city council met for four hours on Tuesday to discuss a range of municipal matters, including parking, signage, and committee appointments. In the final vote of the night, the council approved a 12 percent increase in water rates.

critiques.text_quote: In the final vote of the night, the council approved a 12 percent increase in water rates
critiques.issue_label: the news is buried at the bottom
critiques.question: The thing that actually affects every reader, a 12 percent rise in their water bill, is the last sentence, after parking and signage. Why is the reader expected to wade through the agenda to find the news?
critiques.why_it_matters: When the lede misses the actual news, most readers never reach it. The water rate increase is the story; the four-hour meeting is the setting.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Lead with the water rate increase and what it costs a household. The procedural detail can follow.

source: Plain-language practice; the ambiguous pronoun between two named people
article: Senator Calhoun met the governor at the state capitol on Friday, where he urged him to call a special session.

critiques.text_quote: he urged him to call a special session
critiques.question: 'He urged him.' Both men are 'he'. Who urged whom? A reader cannot tell from the sentence which one wants the special session.
critiques.why_it_matters: When two named people of the same gender share a paragraph and a pronoun follows, about half of readers pick the wrong one. The factual meaning of the sentence is genuinely unclear.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Replace at least one pronoun with the title or name.
critiques.replacement: where the senator urged the governor to call a special session

source: Plain-language practice; context a beat reporter forgets the reader needs
article: After last week's indictment, the company's shares fell sharply and three executives resigned, the biggest upheaval in the firm's 40-year history.

critiques.text_quote: After last week's indictment
critiques.question: Whose indictment, and on what charges? A reader who has not been following the story is dropped into the middle of it. One clause of context would carry them.
critiques.why_it_matters: Assuming the reader has tracked the case is a beat reporter's blind spot. Without the who and the what, the rest of the sentence does not land.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Add the who and the charge so the sentence stands on its own for a first-time reader.
critiques.replacement: After the company's chief executive was indicted last week on fraud charges

source: Plain-language practice; scan for 'was, were, by, been' as passive signals
article: The safety report was shelved for two years and was only released after a freedom of information request.

critiques.text_quote: The safety report was shelved for two years
critiques.issue_label: passive voice hides who shelved it
critiques.question: Shelved by whom? 'Was shelved' tells the reader something was buried but not who buried it. If we know which office or official sat on the report, that is the accountability the sentence is missing.
critiques.why_it_matters: When the passive hides a known actor, the reader cannot assign responsibility. 'Was shelved' reads as if the report shelved itself.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Name the actor if we know it. If we genuinely do not, say the report was shelved and that it is not yet clear who decided to.

source: Orwell's rule against jargon where an everyday word exists; plain-language practice
article: The agency said it would utilise additional resources to facilitate the remediation of the affected sites.

critiques.text_quote: utilise additional resources to facilitate the remediation of the affected sites
critiques.question: 'Utilise', 'facilitate', 'remediation'. Every one of these has an everyday English word that means the same thing. Why is the sentence written in the agency's language instead of the reader's?
critiques.why_it_matters: Bureaucratic phrasing is not more precise, it is just harder. Each jargon word is a small toll the reader pays, and enough of them and the reader stops.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Translate it. 'Use', 'help', 'cleanup'. Say what the agency will actually do in words a reader uses.
critiques.replacement: the agency said it would spend more to help clean up the affected sites

source: Australian Press Council adjudication, Australian Financial Review (headline misrepresenting workers' salaries)
article: 390,000 DOLLAR TUGBOAT WORKERS TO STRIKE FOR 40 PERCENT RISE. The deckhands, whose base pay is far lower, are seeking a phased increase, with the 390,000 figure reflecting only a handful of the most senior masters working maximum overtime.

critiques.text_quote: 390,000 DOLLAR TUGBOAT WORKERS TO STRIKE FOR 40 PERCENT RISE
critiques.question: The headline says '390,000 dollar tugboat workers', but your own text says that figure is a handful of senior masters on maximum overtime, and the deckhands earn far less. The headline describes the rare case as if it were the rule.
critiques.why_it_matters: The Australian Press Council upheld a complaint over an Australian Financial Review headline that misrepresented workers' salaries this way. A headline built on the outlier number leaves every reader with the wrong picture.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Headline the typical pay, not the maximum. If the high figure is newsworthy, explain in the text who actually earns it.
critiques.replacement: TUGBOAT CREWS TO STRIKE OVER PAY, SENIOR MASTERS CAN EARN UP TO 390,000 DOLLARS

source: Plain-language practice; loaded verbs of attribution
article: Mason claimed yesterday that the firm had cleared the new accounting standards. The auditors said they had.

critiques.text_quote: Mason claimed yesterday that the firm had cleared the new accounting standards
critiques.question: Mason 'claimed', the auditors 'said'. The pair of verbs tells the reader to doubt one and trust the other. If we have reason to doubt Mason, the reporting should show it. If we do not, 'said' is the neutral verb for both.
critiques.why_it_matters: Stylebooks (AP, Reuters, Guardian) consistently warn against 'claimed' as a verb of attribution in news writing because it signals reporter disbelief in the speaker. The choice is editorial and the reader picks it up.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use 'said' for both unless we have direct evidence to contradict Mason and can show that evidence in the same paragraph.
critiques.replacement: Mason said yesterday that the firm had cleared the new accounting standards, an account the auditors confirmed

source: Plain-language practice; numbers reported without units or comparators
article: The new car-sharing service has 12,000 vehicles in its fleet, growing daily.

critiques.text_quote: 12,000 vehicles in its fleet, growing daily
critiques.question: 12,000 vehicles is the total. Is that a lot? It depends what city we are talking about and what the competitors run. Without a comparator, the number is decoration.
critiques.why_it_matters: Numbers without scale do not help the reader weigh the story. They sit in the sentence looking precise without telling anyone anything actionable.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Anchor the figure to something the reader can hold: total city taxis, the nearest competitor's fleet, the figure a year ago.
critiques.replacement: 12,000 vehicles, about twice the size of the city's taxi fleet and double the company's number a year ago

source: Plain-language practice; verbal scaffolding adds words without meaning
article: It is worth noting that, in many cases, the agency has, at various points over the past several years, taken what could be described as a fairly cautious approach to enforcement.

critiques.text_quote: It is worth noting that, in many cases, the agency has, at various points over the past several years, taken what could be described as a fairly cautious approach to enforcement
critiques.issue_label: verbal scaffolding burying a simple claim
critiques.question: Strip everything that does not mean anything ('it is worth noting', 'in many cases', 'at various points', 'what could be described as', 'fairly') and what is left? 'The agency has been cautious about enforcement.' That is the sentence. Why are we wrapping it in fog?
critiques.why_it_matters: Verbal scaffolding is the writing equivalent of clearing your throat. Every cushioning phrase is a place where the reader can stop reading.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Cut the cushions. Say the thing.
critiques.replacement: The agency has been cautious about enforcement for years.

source: Plain-language practice; time vagueness
article: Recently, the agency, just months ago, finalised new rules around the disputed practice.

critiques.text_quote: Recently, the agency, just months ago, finalised new rules
critiques.question: Recently, just months ago. Which is it? 'Recently' could be anywhere from a week to a year ago. If we know the date, why are we vague about it?
critiques.why_it_matters: Time-vague writing prevents the reader from connecting events to other things they know about. It also signals that the reporter did not check the date.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use the date or month. 'In March' or 'six weeks ago' beats 'recently' every time.
critiques.replacement: In March, the agency finalised new rules

source: Plain-language practice; quote without attribution
article: 'The administration has clearly chosen to ignore the science.'

critiques.text_quote: 'The administration has clearly chosen to ignore the science.'
critiques.question: Who said this? An unsourced quote sitting on its own tells the reader to take the claim seriously without telling them whose claim it is. Even one name on the end would change the weight of the line.
critiques.why_it_matters: Unattributed quotes are the most basic accountability gap in newswriting. Style guides treat them as a hard fail. The reader cannot weigh a claim if they do not know who is making it.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Attribute the quote, even briefly. If the source insists on anonymity, attribute to a category (a senior scientist at the agency, a former adviser to the president).
critiques.replacement: 'The administration has clearly chosen to ignore the science,' said Dr Eleanor Park, the agency's former chief scientist.

source: Plain-language practice; vague quantifiers
article: Some experts have warned that the rules could create problems, while many others have praised them.

critiques.text_quote: Some experts have warned that the rules could create problems, while many others have praised them
critiques.question: How many is 'some' and how many is 'many'? Two? Twenty? The vague quantifiers do the work of suggesting a divided field without anyone having to count.
critiques.why_it_matters: 'Some' and 'many' are the smallest, easiest words to hide weak reporting behind. Stylebooks consistently flag them. If the field is genuinely divided, we should be able to name three people on each side.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Replace the quantifiers with names or numbers. 'Three of the seven scientists we interviewed' beats 'some experts'.
critiques.replacement: Three of the seven scientists we contacted warned that the rules could create problems; the other four praised them

source: Plain-language practice; the run-on sentence that loses the reader
article: The mayor, who had previously expressed concerns about the project despite earlier statements of support, indicated yesterday that, while she was still considering the implications of the council's vote, which had divided the room and produced a sharp exchange between two members, she might be open to revisiting the question if the funding came through, although she stopped short of committing.

critiques.text_quote: The mayor, who had previously expressed concerns about the project despite earlier statements of support, indicated yesterday that, while she was still considering the implications of the council's vote, which had divided the room and produced a sharp exchange between two members, she might be open to revisiting the question if the funding came through, although she stopped short of committing.
critiques.question: One sentence, eight clauses, and by the end the reader has forgotten what the mayor said. What is the simple version, the version the reader can carry?
critiques.why_it_matters: Single-sentence pile-ups are a documented comprehension failure. Average sentence length in newspaper style guides hovers around 17 words for a reason.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Break it into three sentences. Put the news, the position, and the qualifier each in their own.
critiques.replacement: The mayor said yesterday she might revisit the question if the funding came through. She stopped short of committing. The council's vote on Tuesday had divided the room and produced a sharp exchange between two members.

source: Plain-language practice; 'reportedly' as a fog word
article: The minister reportedly approved the deal during a reportedly tense meeting with reportedly skeptical advisers.

critiques.text_quote: The minister reportedly approved the deal during a reportedly tense meeting with reportedly skeptical advisers
critiques.question: Three 'reportedly's in one sentence. Reported by whom? The word is doing the work of attribution without naming anyone. The reader cannot weigh any of it.
critiques.why_it_matters: 'Reportedly' is the most overused hedge in news writing. AP and Reuters style both discourage stacking it because it tells the reader 'we are not sure who said this' across the whole sentence.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Replace each 'reportedly' with the actual source. Two named officials, a court filing, a leaked email, whatever the basis is.
critiques.replacement: The minister approved the deal during a tense meeting, according to two officials who attended. Three of his advisers had argued against it, the officials said.

source: Plain-language practice; headline-deck misalignment
article: Headline: COUNCIL VOTES TO BAN PLASTIC BAGS. Deck: The 4-3 vote came after lobbying from environmental groups, though enforcement details have not been finalised and the ban will not take effect for at least 18 months.

critiques.text_quote: Headline: COUNCIL VOTES TO BAN PLASTIC BAGS. Deck: The 4-3 vote came after lobbying from environmental groups, though enforcement details have not been finalised and the ban will not take effect for at least 18 months.
critiques.issue_label: headline simpler than the actual news
critiques.question: The headline says the ban is in. The deck says it passed narrowly, has no enforcement plan, and is 18 months from taking effect. Those are different stories, and a reader who only sees the headline thinks the bags are gone.
critiques.why_it_matters: Headlines that overstate the body's complexity are the most-screenshot, most-corrected element in a newspaper. The deck exists to bridge that gap; here it gets to do all the work the headline avoided.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Move the qualifier into the headline. 'Council votes 4-3 for plastic bag ban that will not take effect until 2027' is longer and right.
critiques.replacement: COUNCIL VOTES 4-3 FOR PLASTIC BAG BAN THAT TAKES EFFECT IN 2027

source: Plain-language practice; numbers that switch units mid-paragraph
article: The pipeline runs 142 miles to the storage facility, carrying about 6,000 cubic metres per day, with a planned expansion that would add 50 kilometres next year.

critiques.text_quote: 142 miles to the storage facility, carrying about 6,000 cubic metres per day, with a planned expansion that would add 50 kilometres
critiques.question: Miles, cubic metres, kilometres, all in one paragraph. Which audience is the piece for? A reader doing arithmetic in their head has to convert as they go, and most do not.
critiques.why_it_matters: Style guides are consistent on choosing one system of units per piece, with conversions in parentheses if needed. Mixing systems in the same paragraph is a documented friction point that costs comprehension.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Pick one system. Convert the rest into it. If the audience demands both (international wires), put the converted figure in parentheses each time.
critiques.replacement: The pipeline runs 142 miles to the storage facility, carrying about 6,000 cubic metres (1.6 million gallons) per day, with a planned expansion that would add 31 miles next year

source: Plain-language practice; the legal phrase 'could'
article: The new policy could see refugees deported within 48 hours of arrival.

critiques.text_quote: The new policy could see refugees deported within 48 hours of arrival
critiques.question: 'Could' is doing two jobs. It could mean 'the policy permits' (always true under the rule) or 'this is what we expect to happen in practice' (a prediction). Which is it?
critiques.why_it_matters: Modal verbs like 'could' and 'may' are the most common source of headline-body slippage in policy reporting. Readers tend to read 'will' when we write 'could', and the consequences pile up in corrections.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Replace 'could' with the actual mechanism. If the policy permits 48-hour deportations, say so. If you expect them to happen, say why.
critiques.replacement: Under the new policy, the government would have the legal authority to deport refugees within 48 hours of arrival; officials said the average is expected to be longer.

source: Plain-language practice; the long quote that should be paraphrased
article: 'Look, what I am saying is that the situation as it currently stands is, in many respects, frankly quite different from what we anticipated at the outset of this initiative, which is to say a year ago, and so what we need to do, in my view, is take a step back and reconsider how we are approaching this from, you know, more or less the ground up,' the minister said.

critiques.text_quote: 'Look, what I am saying is that the situation as it currently stands is, in many respects, frankly quite different from what we anticipated at the outset of this initiative, which is to say a year ago, and so what we need to do, in my view, is take a step back and reconsider how we are approaching this from, you know, more or less the ground up,'
critiques.issue_label: long unedited quote drowning the meaning
critiques.question: The quote rambles for 80 words and the news inside it is 'we need to reconsider this'. Why is the reader being asked to wade through the ums?
critiques.why_it_matters: Long quotes that meander are the easiest place for the news to get lost. The reporter's job in the quote is to find the shortest version that captures what the source meant.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Paraphrase the throat-clearing and quote only the part that carries the news.
critiques.replacement: The minister said the situation was 'frankly quite different from what we anticipated' a year ago, and that the initiative needed to be 'reconsidered from the ground up'.

source: Example of a clear lede that leads with the news and its cost to the reader
article: The city will raise water rates by 12 percent in July, adding about 90 dollars to the average annual household bill, the utility said.

source: Example of clear, active attribution that answers who did what
article: The mayor fired the public works director on Monday, citing the department's failure to clear snow from major roads after the storm.

source: Example of a specialist term used appropriately in its specialist context
article: In the bond markets, the 10-year yield rose three basis points, a routine move on a quiet trading day.

source: Example of a sourced, neutral, well-quantified passage
article: Sales of electric vehicles rose 14 percent in the year to June, to 1.2 million units, accounting for about 9 percent of new-car sales nationwide, the industry association said.
